Ebola Analysis and Plotting

2026-02-04

Plotting with the incidence package

onset <- outbreaks::ebola_sim$linelist$date_of_onset

i <- incidence(onset)
plot(i)
## Warning: `aes_string()` was deprecated in ggplot2 3.0.0.
## ℹ Please use tidy evaluation idioms with `aes()`.
## ℹ See also `vignette("ggplot2-in-packages")` for more information.
## ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the incidence package.
##   Please report the issue at <https://github.com/reconhub/incidence/issues>.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

Change appearance

plot(i, color= 'lavender',alpha = 0.8, xlab='Date case reported')

# Add breaks on x-axis

plot(i, color= 'gold',alpha = 0.9, xlab='Date case reported', n_breaks= 10)

# Weekly incidence

i_week <- incidence(onset, interval = 7) #weekly incidence
plot(i_week)

plot(i_week, labels_week = FALSE)

my_fit <- fit(i)
## Warning in fit(i): 22 dates with incidence of 0 ignored for fitting
plot(i, fit= my_fit)

The entire graph doesn’t have exponential growth, it has exponential decay/decline about half way through the displayed period. Hence the fit is off. To solve this the split function should be used.

split_fit <- fit(i, split = as.Date("2014-12-15"))
## Warning in fit(i, split = as.Date("2014-12-15")): 22 dates with incidence of 0
## ignored for fitting
plot(i, fit= split_fit)

Using optim_fit_split, the optimal average fit for before and after can be found. (Selects best date)

optim_split <- fit_optim_split(i)
optim_split
## $df
##         dates   mean.R2
## 1  2014-08-02 0.5789836
## 2  2014-08-03 0.5874561

## 98 2014-11-07 0.7835444
## 99 2014-11-08 0.7802628
## 
## $split
## [1] "2014-09-28"
## 
## $fit
## <list of incidence_fit objects>
## 
## attr(x, 'locations'): list of vectors with the locations of each incidence_fit object
## 
## 'before'
## 'after'
## 
## $model: regression of log-incidence over time
## 
## $info: list containing the following items:
##   $r (daily growth rate):
##      before       after 
##  0.02424425 -0.01012732 
## 
##   $r.conf (confidence interval):
##              2.5 %      97.5 %
## before  0.02269567  0.02579282
## after  -0.01075483 -0.00949980
## 
##   $doubling (doubling time in days):
##   before 
## 28.59017 
## 
##   $doubling.conf (confidence interval):
##           2.5 %   97.5 %
## before 26.87365 30.54094
## 
##   $halving (halving time in days):
##    after 
## 68.44332 
## 
##   $halving.conf (confidence interval):
##          2.5 %  97.5 %
## after 64.44983 72.9644
## 
##   $pred: data.frame of incidence predictions (368 rows, 6 columns)
## 
## $plot

optim_split$split
## [1] "2014-09-28"
optim_fit <- optim_split$fit
plot(i, fit = optim_fit)

Plotting with epiEstim

s_interval <- make_config(
  mean_si= 15.3,
  std_si= 9.3
)
my_R <-estimate_R(i, method="parametric_", config = s_interval)
## Default config will estimate R on weekly sliding windows.
##     To change this change the t_start and t_end arguments.
## Warning in estimate_R_func(incid = incid, method = method, si_sample = si_sample, : You're estimating R too early in the epidemic to get the desired
##             posterior CV.
plot(my_R)
## Warning: Removed 8 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_ribbon()`).
## Warning: Removed 8 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_line()`).

plot(my_R, what= "R", legend = TRUE,
     options_R = list(col="red", xlab= "Date"))
## Warning: Removed 8 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_ribbon()`).
## Warning: Removed 8 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_line()`).

Using ggplot()

sex <- outbreaks::ebola_sim$linelist$gender
cases <- tibble(onset= onset, sex= sex)
cases
## # A tibble: 5,888 × 2
##    onset      sex  
##    <date>     <fct>
##  1 2014-04-07 f    
##  2 2014-04-15 m    
##  3 2014-04-21 f    
##  4 2014-04-27 f    
##  5 2014-04-26 f    
##  6 2014-04-25 f    
##  7 2014-05-03 f    
##  8 2014-05-01 f    
##  9 2014-04-21 m    
## 10 2014-05-05 m    
## # ℹ 5,878 more rows
g <- cases %>% ggplot(aes(x= onset, fill = sex)) + geom_histogram()
g
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.

Add labels

g <- g + labs(title = "Histogram of onset dates", x = "Dates")
g
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.

Change colours

g <- g + scale_fill_manual(values = c('pink','skyblue')) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16)
g
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.

Add legend

g <- g + scale_fill_manual(values = c('pink','skyblue'),
  labels = c('Female', 'Male'))
## Scale for fill is already present.
## Adding another scale for fill, which will replace the existing scale.
g
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.
